The core principle is to exploit the azimuthal structure difference between focused targets and interference: the target response manifests as a sinc function, while the interference response exhibits a linear frequency-modulated (LFM) form. Specifically, the method segments images into patches, performs the singular value decomposition (SVD) on each patch, applies the fractional Fourier transform (FRFT) at the optimal angle to the left singular vector matrix, and realizes constant false alarm rate (CFAR) detection by comparing the squared global FRFT peak,  $p_{1}$ , with an adaptive threshold. Theoretical derivation shows that  $p_{1}$  obeys the Gumbel distribution in clean SAR images, providing a basis for adaptive threshold calculation. For the terrain observation by progressive scans (TOPS) mode, the linear variation of the focused target Doppler bandwidth centroid degrades detection performance; thus, an interference model for the TOPS mode is established, and detection preprocessing tailored to this mode is introduced to enhance detection robustness. Experiments on measured data from Gaofen-3 and Sentinel-1 demonstrate that SFID can effectively detect low interference-to-signal ratio (ISR) and dense interference, outperforming traditional detection methods.
author: Lunhao Duan, Xingyu Lu, Jianchao Yang, Huizhang Yang, Hong Gu

title: Scattering-Aware Diffusion Model: Enforcing Polarimetric Fidelity for PolSAR Data Generation
content: Polarimetric synthetic aperture radar (PolSAR) provides physically interpretable observations through polarization-dependent scattering responses. Developing reliable PolSAR interpretation methods is often constrained by data scarcity due to limited coverage of acquisition conditions and scene states. Generative models can mitigate this scarcity, but most treat PolSAR as generic multichannel imagery and neglect interchannel polarimetric correlations that encode scattering characteristics. Consequently, generated samples may be visually plausible yet exhibit limited polarimetric fidelity, reducing their utility for PolSAR interpretation. To address this issue, we propose a scattering-aware diffusion model (SADM) that enforces scattering consistency to improve polarimetric fidelity. Specifically, we devise a scattering-decoupling strategy within SADM that learns representations for each polarimetric channel independently to preserve physical meaning and captures scattering mechanisms via interchannel correlations among these representations. This strategy then employs the resulting scattering descriptors to modulate the spatial features in the main generation pathway, endowing them with explicit scattering semantics. Furthermore, we incorporate a scattering mechanism consistency loss to facilitate learning the joint distribution of PolSAR data and polarimetric features, enhancing polarimetric fidelity beyond pixel-level appearance. Experiments on a publicly available Ku-band full-polarimetric 10-class civilian vehicle dataset (KuPolVehicle-10) show that SADM achieves a Yamaguchi-based polarimetric Fréchet inception distance (Pol-FID) of 3.506 versus 5.245 for a standard denoising diffusion probabilistic model (DDPM) baseline. Moreover, SADM-based augmentation improves classification accuracy by 4.94%, indicating that enforcing polarimetric fidelity translates to improved utility in PolSAR interpretation. Additional evaluations on OpenSARShip 2.0 confirm that SADM generalizes well across polarization modes, frequency bands, and target classes.
author: Jiyuan Liu, Wei Wang, Tao Zhang, Dehui Zhu, Hongqi Fan

title: Transformer-Based Modeling of Global Microwave Land Surface Emissivity Using GPM
content: Global land surface microwave emissivity estimates are critical for data assimilation, numerical weather prediction, and quantitative precipitation estimation, yet explicit calculations at multiple frequencies remain problematic. We introduce a machine learning model that converts 10.65-GHz H-polarization emissivity retrieved under clear-sky conditions into emissivity for the 12 higher-frequency channels of the global precipitation measurement (GPM) microwave imager (GMI). The model is a feature-tokenizer transformer that ingests ancillary surface variables such as skin temperature, leaf area index, soil texture, and soil moisture. Treating each variable as an individual token lets the transformer direct attention between features and learn nonlinear, spatiotemporally consistent relationships across five years of data [2015–2019]. The network reproduces channel-specific emissivities with  $R^{2} \gt 0.80$  and mean absolute percentage error <1%, and it simulates top-of-atmosphere brightness temperatures with  $R^{2} = 0.77{\,}-{\,}0.95$  and mean biases <4 K for every channel up to 89 GHz when compared to collocated GMI observations. Using the independent Tool to Estimate Land Surface Emissivities at Microwave Frequencies Version 2 (TELSEM2) 10.65-GHz H-pol inputs produces similar skill, suggesting that the learned mapping is robust to the input source, and captures transferable physical structures. The model is lightweight and can be embedded in satellite retrieval systems, reanalysis pipelines, and Earth-system models to accelerate microwave forward modeling and improve global precipitation monitoring.
author: Fraser King, Sarah Ringerud

title: APOFormer: Adaptive Geometric Primitive Optimization Transformer for Building Polygon Extraction From Remote Sensing Imagery
content: The automatic generation of building polygons from remote sensing imagery has significant applications but confronts challenges due to complex urban scenes, frequent occlusions, multiscale features, and diverse geometric morphologies. Segmentation-based methods are constrained by irregular boundaries and imprecise contours, often requiring extensive postprocessing. Geometric primitive-based approaches typically overlook geometrical primitive interdependencies and contextual semantic correlations, causing suboptimal accuracy and topological errors. To address these issues, this study proposed a novel approach, Adaptive Geometric Primitive Optimization Transformer (APOFormer), for building polygon extraction from remote sensing imagery, combining the advantages of the convolution neural network (CNN) and Transformer. APOFormer represents polygons as vertex-query-based sequences and establishes interactive mechanisms for primitive correlations using positional embedding and image context. In feature extraction, the CNN encoder incorporated learnable corner-edge detection operators to explicitly enhance building primitive features. Additionally, a dynamic vertex query mechanism and the multi-vertex collaborative deformable attention (DA) module are developed to improve position prediction accuracy and efficiency using the Transformer-based decoder. Furthermore, an edge-context-guided topology reconstruction (ECTR) module is constructed to enhance polygon quality. Comprehensive experimental evaluation on WHU, INRIA, and MASB datasets, validated by visualization and qualitative analyses, demonstrates the superiority of APOFormer, achieving an average of 83.79% Average Precision (AP), 27.73 MTA, 88.01% C-IoU, and 93.90% IoU. APOFormer achieves remarkable generalization ability and provides robust solutions for accurate building polygon generation.
author: Qinglie Yuan

title: From Dual-Stream Manifold-Aligned Tokenizer to Latent Sensor-Invariant Consistency Model: Rapid Generative Super-Resolution for Cross-Sensor Remote Sensing Imagery
content: Diffusion models have established state-of-the-art visual realism in remote sensing super-resolution (SR) but suffer from high inference latency. Although accelerated approaches such as latent consistency models (LCMs) enable efficient one-step sampling in a compressed space, their effectiveness in cross-sensor scenarios is hindered by latent manifold misalignment. This limitation arises from the magnified distributional discrepancy within the compressed latent space, which undermines the stability of consistency training and hinders convergence on heterogeneous data. To address this challenge, we propose a unified framework that explicitly decouples cross-sensor SR into sensor-invariant manifold learning and single-step latent residual generation. First, to effectively bridge the domain gap, we introduce the dual-stream Sinkhorn-contrastive masked autoencoder (DSSC-MAE). Functioning as a domain-aligned tokenizer, it employs a novel hierarchical alignment strategy that integrates reconstruction objectives with global Sinkhorn optimal transport (OT) and local patch-wise contrastive constraints. This design aligns heterogeneous distributions within a unified, structure-preserving latent manifold, effectively mitigating sensor-specific discrepancies. Second, building upon this aligned latent space, we propose the latent sensor-invariant residual consistency model (LSIRCM). Leveraging the sensor-invariant latent representation as a structural anchor, LSIRCM reformulates SR as a controllable latent residual generation task. By incorporating cross-attention mechanisms to integrate this aligned context, it utilizes an anchor Diffusion Transformer (AnchorDiT) backbone with a hybrid consistency training strategy to condense the iterative sampling trajectory into a deterministic single-step mapping. Extensive experiments on public and newly constructed datasets demonstrate that our approach achieves a superior efficiency–quality tradeoff. It surpasses the perceptual quality of state-of-the-art diffusion models while maintaining high structural fidelity, matching the inference speed of regression-based networks. Consequently, our framework offers a robust and computationally efficient solution for cross-sensor remote sensing SR tasks. The code will be publicly available at https://github.com/afomyj/LSIRCM
author: Yujie Mao, Guojin He, Guizhou Wang, Ranyu Yin, Hongxuan Zhang, Ziying Chen

title: HDGMamba: High-Frequency Dynamic Guided Mamba for Robust Multispectral-Hyperspectral Image Fusion
content: Multispectral-hyperspectral image fusion (MHIF) generates high-resolution hyperspectral images (HRHSIs) by integrating the spectral information from low-resolution hyperspectral images (LRHSIs) with spatial details from high-resolution multispectral images (HRMSIs). Recently, researchers have focused on balancing fusion performance and computational efficiency, driving the adoption of Mamba-based architectures in MHIF. However, source images often suffer from various types of noise introduced by sensor imperfections and transmission conditions. While conventional denoising techniques can improve image quality, they often cause spatial blurring, which is an issue further amplified in Mamba due to its limited ability to model local features. To address these challenges, we propose HDGMamba, a high-frequency dynamic guided Mamba (HDGMamba) for robust MHIF tasks under noisy conditions. Its core, the high-frequency modulated state space model (HM-SSM), fuses spatial–spectral information by modeling global relationships. Crucially, HM-SSM dynamically modulates the state update process via high-frequency spatial variations to enhance fine-grained local details. Furthermore, to mitigate misleading fusion caused by noise of high-frequency features, a high-frequency gate module (HGM) is developed to extract clean high-frequency features by exploiting the distinctive gradient patterns of stochastic noise. Extensive experiments on multiple benchmark datasets demonstrate that HDGMamba significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods in terms of both fusion accuracy and noise robustness.
author: Jiangtao Huang, Ting Luo, Zhouyan He, Haiyong Xu, Yang Song, Leyuan Fang

title: Orthogonal Rational Basis-Function-Based Discriminant Analysis for Hyperspectral Image Classification
content: This article presents a nonlinear discriminant analysis (NDA) method based on an orthogonal rational basis function (ORF) system for the classification of hyperspectral images (HSIs). The proposed method, abbreviated ORF-NDA, is formulated from the standpoint of function theory. It can make good use of the spectral curve characteristics of HSIs and establish a methodology more suitable for HSI processing in the function space. This is a new paradigm that is different from mainstream hyperspectral data processing methods. First, a functional data fitting model based on an adaptive ORF system is designed for the reconstruction of the spectral curve for each pixel in HSIs. Second, the stochastic adaptive Fourier decomposition (SAFD) method is used to find the optimal parameters for building the adaptive ORF system. Third, based on the reconstructed spectral curve, an NDA method based on an ORF system is built for feature extraction of HSIs. Finally, a spectral–spatial classification framework is designed for HSIs in scenarios with limited sample sizes. Experimental results on five public HSIs demonstrate that the proposed ORF-NDA method outperforms several state-of-the-art deep learning methods in classification accuracy.
author: Zhijing Ye, Liming Zhang, Jiangtao Peng, Jón Atli Benediktsson, Tao Qian

title: DeTracker: Motion-Decoupled Vehicle Detection and Tracking in Unstabilized Satellite Videos
content: Satellite videos provide continuous observations of surface dynamics but pose significant challenges for multiobject tracking (MOT), especially under unstabilized conditions where platform jitter and the weak appearance of tiny objects jointly degrade tracking performance. To address this problem, we propose DeTracker, a joint-detection-and-tracking (JDT) framework tailored for unstabilized satellite videos. DeTracker introduces a task-driven global–local motion decoupling (GLMD) module to address the motion imbalance between dominant platform motion and weak target motion. It suppresses background-dominated motion via global semantic alignment at the feature level and captures target-specific motion through local refinement (LR), improving trajectory stability and identity consistency. In addition, a temporal dependency feature pyramid (TDFP) module is developed to perform cross-frame temporal feature fusion, enhancing the continuity and discriminability of tiny-object representations. We further construct a new benchmark dataset, SDM-Car-SU, which simulates multidirectional and multispeed platform motions to enable systematic evaluation of tracking robustness under varying motion perturbations. Extensive experiments on both simulated and real unstabilized satellite videos demonstrate that DeTracker significantly outperforms existing methods, achieving 61.1% MOTA on SDM-Car-SU and 45.3% MOTA on real satellite video data. The code and dataset will be publicly available at https://github.com/alex-chenjiajun/DeTracker
author: Jiajun Chen, Jing Xiao, Shaohan Cao, Yuming Zhu, Liang Liao, Jun Pan, Mi Wang

title: DepthGeoLoc: Learning Structure-Aware Depth Priors for Robust UAV-Satellite Cross-View Geo-Localization
content: Recent advances in cross-view geo-localization (CVGL) serve as a critical foundation for aerial vehicles (UAVs) to deliver reliable remote sensing applications in GNSS-denied complex environments. However, current CVGL methods primarily focus on RGB images, neglecting the potential advantages of incorporating additional modalities. In this article, we propose DepthGeoLoc, a novel multimodal training CVGL framework that integrates depth-aware structural features to address the significant cross-view disparity challenges. Concretely, we propose two key innovations: 1) a depth-aware feature fusion (DAFF) module to enable joint geometry-semantics learning of target landmarks, effectively bridging geometric depth cues with high-level semantic representations; and 2) moreover, inspired by the depth distribution patterns of salient regions in real-world urban scenarios captured from UAV and satellite perspectives, we propose two novel components: regional depth distribution masking (RDDM) and target region structural alignment (TRSA). These mechanisms synergistically address the cross-view alignment challenges by leveraging depth correlation priors. Benefiting from our proposed coarse-to-fine knowledge distillation paradigm, we achieve a favorable balance between accuracy and computational efficiency. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our multimodal training framework significantly outperforms the state-of-the-art models. The depth-distilled version of our DepthGeoLoc achieves  ${1.5} \times $  higher throughput (versus SDPL) and a 52.68% reduction in FLOPs (versus Safe-Net), while maintaining competitive performance.
author: Jian Sun, Kangdao Liu, Chen-Bin Feng, Jin Wu, Weiping Ding, Chi-Man Vong

title: MGRT-Net: Multi-Gain Radiometric Response Transfer Network for On-Orbit Relative Radiometric Calibration of Micro Nighttime Remote Sensing Satellites
content: Inconsistent radiometric response characteristics of nighttime sensor detectors can interfere with weak nighttime surface radiation, resulting in radiometric inconsistencies in captured images. This degrades image quality and compromises information extraction from such image products. Relative radiometric calibration involves calibrating response differences among imaging detectors to eliminate radiometric inconsistencies, which is particularly critical for nighttime sensors. However, existing on-orbit calibration methods require onboard calibration devices, reliable attitude control, and dedicated calibration imaging tasks. The limited hardware capabilities and attitude control capacity of micro nighttime satellites pose significant challenges for radiometric calibration. This study proposes a deep learning-based on-orbit relative radiometric calibration method for nighttime sensors to address these limitations, representing an effort to apply deep learning techniques to relative radiometric calibration of remote sensing satellites. A Multi-Gain Radiometric Response Transfer Network (MGRT-Net) was developed to characterize the radiometric response relationships of nighttime sensors across gain settings. A daytime low-gain radiometric calibration reference was established using a uniform calibration method. The calibration reference is transferred to nighttime images to calibrate the radiometric parameters of the nighttime sensor in high-gain mode. Using Luojia1-01 nighttime images, MGRT-Net accurately simulated radiometric responses across different gain settings and effectively transferred daytime low-gain radiometric calibration references to nighttime images. After MGRT-Net–based transfer correction, radiometric response errors among sensor detectors in nighttime high-gain images were effectively eliminated. Under low- and high-brightness, the streaking metrics (SMs) exceeded 0.027% (76.5% improvement) and 0.028% (75.6% improvement), respectively, outperforming existing methods. Compared with analytical calibration methods, the proposed method requires no prior model or additional satellite requirements and demonstrates strong adaptability. This study provides a reference for micro-remote sensing satellites constrained by manufacturing costs and validates the applicability of deep learning methods for relative radiometric calibration.
author: Litao Li, Jiayang Cao, Yonghua Jiang, Chengjun Wang, Xin Shen

title: TBR-Mamba: Hierarchical Transfer Learning and Boundary-Aware Spatiotemporal Interaction Network for Forest Change Detection
content: High-resolution forest change detection (FCD) is essential for carbon assessment and sustainable forest management, yet reliable detection in complex multitemporal scenes remains difficult because phenology-induced pseudo-changes, irregular and blurred boundaries, and unstable model adaptation under limited supervision jointly degrade performance. In this work, we propose temporal and boundary-refined Mamba (TBR-Mamba), a Siamese state-space framework tailored for high-resolution FCD. To support this task, we further construct Hunan forest change detection (HNF-CD), a public, large-scale, high-resolution benchmark for complex multitemporal forest scenes, featuring expert-guided annotation of semantically verified cross-category transitions while retaining phenology-related and other nonsemantic hard negatives. To handle temporal discrepancies, the phenology-resistant temporal contrast (PRTC) module suppresses phenology-induced pseudo-changes through temporal contrast-guided attention (TCGA) and asymmetric refinement (AR) in a dual-stream interaction scheme. To resolve spatial ambiguity, the edge-prior guided decoupling (EPGD) module sharpens blurred boundaries by decoupling boundary cues from region semantics under boundary-aware joint optimization. Finally, to stabilize convergence under limited annotations, the hierarchical transfer fine-tuning encoder (HTFT-E) adapts the Swin-UMamba backbone through stagewise transfer control, promoting effective transfer of generic visual priors to forestry-specific semantics. Experiments on HNF-CD, Forest-CD, and Zhejiang-CD show that TBR-Mamba achieves the best overall performance among the compared methods, reaching  $F1$ /IoU of the change class (cIoU) scores of 89.81%/81.50%, 95.14%/90.74%, and 92.60%/86.23%, respectively. Cross-dataset transfer experiments further suggest that HNF-CD serves as a useful forestry-specific intermediate source, complementing generic ImageNet pretraining. Overall, these results indicate that TBR-Mamba is effective for fine-grained FCD in heterogeneous forest environments.
author: Jingxiang Yuan, Xiaobo Tan, Gufeng Gong, Guoxiong Zhou, Ming'e Zhu

title: MSGFusion: Multimodal Scene Graph-Guided Infrared and Visible Image Fusion
content: Infrared and visible image fusion (IVIF) aims to generate high-quality images with both rich low-level details and clear high-level semantics. However, prevailing data-driven approaches remain largely confined to interactions at the level of low-level features, such as pixel intensities or gradients. Although some task-driven methods introduce semantic constraints via downstream labels, such as object detection or semantic segmentation, they are limited to implicit priors or local regions, struggling to provide global, context-aware structured semantic guidance. Recent text-driven methods attempt to incorporate unstructured textual priors; however, directly injecting them into low-level fusion networks often disrupts the inherent spatial and semantic coherence, ultimately leading to edge distortion and texture degradation. We therefore present MSGFusion, a novel multimodal scene graph-guided image fusion framework designed to bridge the representation gap between high-level semantics and the low-level pixel space. By deeply coupling structured scene graphs derived from both text and vision, MSGFusion explicitly represents entities, attributes, and spatial relations. It subsequently aligns the parsed structured semantics to specific local visual regions through successive modules for scene graph representation, hierarchical aggregation, and scene graph-driven fusion. This framework reformulates traditional pixel-level aggregation into spatially associated and semantically aware fine-grained fusion. Extensive experiments on multiple public benchmarks confirm that MSGFusion significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods, yielding prominent advantages in detail preservation and structural clarity. Furthermore, the proposed method not only exhibits outstanding semantic consistency in downstream tasks like pedestrian detection and semantic segmentation but also features high computational efficiency and strong cross-domain generalization capabilities in medical image fusion tasks. Code is available at: https://github.com/Kazdong628/MSGFusion
author: Guihui Li, Bowei Dong, Kaizhi Dong, Jiayi Li, Haiyong Zheng

title: EntSeg: Entropy-Guided Pseudolabel Denoising and Masked Image Consistency for Cross-Domain Remote Sensing Segmentation
content: Unsupervised domain adaptation (UDA) for semantic segmentation of remote sensing images (RSIs) aims to adapt a model trained on labeled source-domain data to a target domain without requiring target-domain annotations. Existing self-training (ST) approaches adapt the model by generating pseudolabels for target-domain samples. However, due to the inherent domain gap, these pseudolabels often contain substantial noise, which in turn limits the effectiveness of such methods. We observe that noisy pseudolabels typically exhibit higher entropy. Motivated by this observation, we propose an entropy-guided segmentation (EntSeg) framework, a novel entropy-guided framework for cross-domain RSIs semantic segmentation that comprises two key models. The entropy-weighted pseudolabel denoising (EPLD) module dynamically adjusts the learning weights of noisy pixel locations (i.e., pixels with high entropy) to mitigate the adverse influence of noisy pseudolabels on domain adaptation. Nevertheless, the dynamic weighting mechanism of EPLD also results in insufficient contextual learning for target-domain data, especially at locations assigned low weights. To address this issue, we introduce entropy-guided masked image consistency (EMIC), which employs an entropy-guided strategy to mask high-entropy regions and learns spatial contextual relationships in the target domain through masked image consistency, thereby enhancing the model’s robustness in visual recognition. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method achieves highly competitive performance. The code will be released at https://github.com/woldier/EntSeg
author: Bin Wang, Zeyu Chen, Shun Lv, Fei Deng, Yiguang Liu

title: Unsupervised Building Change Detection in Remote Sensing Images via Joint Structural and Nonstructural Variation Generation
content: Remote sensing change detection (CD) plays a vital role in urban expansion analysis, ecological assessment, and disaster monitoring. While deep-learning-based methods have significantly advanced performance, they often rely on large-scale multitemporal datasets with pixel-level annotations. To alleviate this dependence, generating paired bitemporal remote sensing images have been explored, but its effectiveness is limited in the absence of pixel-level semantic masks. Moreover, most generation-based CD methods focus solely on modeling structural changes, such as the construction or removal of buildings while neglecting nonstructural variations (i.e., changes caused by illumination shifts, atmospheric fluctuations, or seasonal dynamics that do not alter the physical structure of objects). In this article, we propose SNV-GenCD, an unsupervised CD framework for buildings in high-resolution optical remote sensing images, which generates pseudobitemporal remote sensing images by jointly modeling structural changes and nonstructural variations. Within SNV-GenCD, a structural change generation module produces object-manipulated images along with corresponding pixel-level change masks, and a nonstructural variation module injects radiometric changes that reflect real-world remote sensing conditions. Furthermore, to eliminate temporal bias and enhance robustness against pseudochanges in the generated data, we introduce two complementary constraints that guide the detector to learn robust, order-invariant representations. Experiments on the LEVIR-CD, WHU-CD, and GoogleCD datasets show that SNV-GenCD achieves  $F1$  scores of 82.2%, 87.6%, and 76.7%, respectively. These results significantly outperform existing state-of-the-art (SOTA) unsupervised methods and are competitive with weakly supervised approaches. The code is available at https://github.com/Shakeyoo/SNV-GenCD
author: Yaoyao Wang, Jialei Yu, Han Wang, Xinxiao Wu

title: Utilizing In-Orbit CYGNSS Roll Maneuvers for Improved Level-1 Calibration—Part II: Application to Reflected Signal Channels
content: This work develops a methodology for using commanded roll maneuvers by Cyclone Global Navigation Satellite System (CYGNSS) constellation observatories to improve the determination of the nadir antenna patterns that receive GNSS signals reflected from Earth’s surface. The method relies on observation geometry properties in differing roll states that are used as a diagnostic tool to reveal Level-1 calibration errors associated with prelaunch mischaracterization of the nadir antenna patterns and as numerical constraints on algorithms to derive improved patterns that are more representative of in-orbit gain levels. It is shown that the updated gain estimates differ by −3 to 3 dB in a nonuniform fashion. In the companion work (Part III), the effects the use of these updated patterns has in terms of advancing the consistency of the calibration quality of observables is explored. Initial assessments presented here highlight how observable anomalies attributable to inconsistencies in the characterization of in-orbit nadir may be reduced by over 50% using the proposed methodologies.
author: Mohammad M. Al-Khaldi, Scott Gleason, Christopher S. Ruf, Joel T. Johnson, Darren S. McKague, Anthony Russel, William L. Wells, Tianlin Wang, Dorina Twigg, Patrick McEnaney, Joshua Synowiec

title: TFFNet: Time–Frequency Feature Fusion for Seismic P-Wave First-Arrival Picking in Complex Geological Environments
content: Recent advancements in seismic first-arrival picking have been driven by U-Net-based networks, yet their performance often degrades in low-SNR environments and complex subsurface conditions, where noise and interfering waves (e.g., reflections) obscure weak arrivals. Conventional approaches, relying predominantly on time-domain features, suffer from insufficient representation, limiting their accuracy. To address these challenges, we propose a time–frequency fusion network (TFFNet), a novel U-Net architecture that effectively integrates time- and frequency-domain features to enhance picking performance. At its core is the TFFConv module, which employs parallel branches within a time–frequency attention fusion (TFAF) framework. The frequency branch leverages a Haar-discrete-wavelet-transform (DWT)-based directional decomposition, augmented by learnable weights and frequency-domain attention (FDA), to suppress noise adaptively and amplify first-arrival signals. The time branch employs patch attention to extract task-relevant features. These branches are optimally combined via the power-averaged channel fusion (PACF) module. Comprehensive experiments on three low-SNR field datasets (Lalor, Qaidam, and Xiong’an) demonstrate TFFNet’s efficacy, outperforming four state-of-the-art methods (U-Net benchmark, STU-Net, DSU-Net, and U-Net3+). TFFNet achieves hit rate within 1-pixel error (HR@1px) of 90.9%, 79.6%, and 56.0% on Lalor, Qaidam, and Xiong’an, respectively, with improvements of up to 23.2% on the challenging Xiong’an dataset. These results underscore TFFNet’s potential for precise seismic first-arrival picking in noisy and complex geological settings. Codes are available at https://github.com/WHOAREYOUXSC/TFFNet
author: Jing Wang, Sicheng Xia, Yue Liu, Kai Wang, Xuehua Zhou

title: First-Arrival Traveltime Tomography Based on Fractional-Order Total Variation Regularization
content: First-arrival traveltime tomography has been widely used for near-surface velocity modeling, yet it often yields unsatisfactory inverted results in regions with insufficient ray coverage, abrupt velocity changes, or smoothly varying velocity structures. Conventional regularization techniques are commonly employed to address such limitations, yet they tend to blur sharp velocity interfaces and reduce inversion accuracy. Fractional order derivatives are capable of preserving sharp velocity boundaries and mitigating the staircase effect, but the selection and optimization of the order are computationally expensive. To overcome these drawbacks, we propose an adaptive fractional order total-variation (AFOTV) regularization framework for first-arrival traveltime tomography. The proposed method can not only avoid manual tuning of the fractional order but also ensure inversion accuracy, stability, and efficiency. The objective function is decomposed into two subproblems, which are efficiently solved by the adjoint-state method with preillumination compensation and the split Bregman algorithm, respectively. Both synthetic data examples and field data applications demonstrate that, compared with the conventional inverted method and other regularized methods, the AFOTV method delivers a higher-resolution velocity model with better preserved velocity boundaries and fewer artifacts. When used as an initial model for full-waveform inversion (FWI), it also accelerates convergence while maintaining comparable accuracy. The proposed method provides an effective tool for velocity modeling under more complex near-surface conditions.
author: Qin Sun, Bingshou He, Huixing Zhang

title: Efficient Nonlocal Tensor Function Representation for 3-D Seismic Random Noise Attenuation
content: The high-dimensional 3-D seismic data are inevitably contaminated by random noise due to environmental interference and acquisition system limitations. While many deep learning methods have been proposed for random noise attenuation, they often suffer from various limitations. For instance, some convolutional neural network (CNN)-based methods tend to be computationally inefficient when processing 3-D seismic volumes, while the implicit neural representation (INR)-based methods frequently fail to recover complex structural details due to the spectral bias. To address these challenges, this study introduces an efficient nonlocal tensor function representation (NLTFR) for unsupervised 3-D seismic denoising. Specifically, we cluster similar cubes within 3-D seismic data into nonlocal similar groups. To efficiently and effectively exploit the nonlocal self-similarity inherent in the seismic data, we employ the tensor function factorization parameterized by INRs to model these clustered nonlocal similar groups. Furthermore, we propose a partially shared transfer learning strategy for NLTFR to accelerate the model convergence when applied to large-scale seismic datasets. Multiple-frequency sinusoidal functions are incorporated into the INRs to mitigate the spectral bias, thereby enhancing the model’s representation ability. In addition, a weighted total variation and a second-order 3-D total variation are introduced to improve the denoising performance and robustness of the NLTFR model. Comprehensive experiments conducted on synthetic and field 3-D seismic datasets demonstrate that the proposed NLTFR achieves a superior balance between efficiency and effectiveness compared with conventional deep learning methods. Overall, our method attains an average performance gain of 1.5 dB while significantly reducing execution time relative to traditional deep learning methods for 3-D seismic volumes, underscoring its practical efficiency.
author: Shengrui Wang, Yisi Luo, Bangyu Wu, Jiangjun Peng, Yueming Ye, Junxiong Jia

title: DSRF-SAR: Neural Radiance Field Representation Based on a Dual SAR Scattering Mechanism
content: Synthetic aperture radar (SAR) enables high-resolution imaging of the Earth’s surface under all weather and illumination conditions. However, the high cost of data acquisition and the limited diversity of viewpoints restrict its application in data-intensive tasks. Neural radiance fields (NeRFs) can implicitly represent scenes and synthesize novel-view images from unseen viewpoints. Existing NeRF-based methods have achieved promising results in SAR image generation. However, they often fail to capture the complex electromagnetic scattering characteristics and sharp geometric transitions. This limitation often results in blurring of target regions and the omission of background textures. To address these issues, we propose DSRF-SAR, a NeRF representation based on a dual SAR scattering mechanism. Specifically, the scattering is decomposed into two components: isotropic scattering, which depends solely on spatial location, and anisotropic scattering, which depends on both location and observation geometry. In addition, directional interpolation is reparameterized by using the reflection direction of radar wave instead of the incident direction to learn a more accurate SAR scattering distribution. And the target surface is represented using a signed distance function, enabling stronger geometric constraint and higher-fidelity scene reconstruction. Qualitative and quantitative experiments, including sparse-view interpolation and few-shot extrapolation, are conducted on the moving and stationary target acquisition and recognition dataset. The results demonstrate that the proposed method outperforms existing approaches and generates high-quality SAR images of novel views, providing additional support for SAR image interpretation and applications.
author: Yang Yang, Yueting Zhang, Jiayi Guo, Guangyao Zhou, Xia Ning, Yongchao Zhao

title: Image Dehazing of Unmanned Aerial Vehicles Based on Physics-Guided Diffusion Model
content: Drone vision is often degraded by cloud occlusion, which causes blurring or loss of surface observation information and significantly hinders subsequent image processing and interpretation tasks. Notably, fine details of ground objects can sometimes remain partially visible beneath thin clouds. Meanwhile, recent advances in diffusion models have demonstrated strong generative capabilities, making them promising candidates for addressing thin cloud occlusion. In this article, we propose a physics-guided image dehazing framework for drone vision based on diffusion model. The framework operates in two stages. In the first stage, a multiscale nonlocal U-Net, guided by the atmospheric scattering model, is designed to estimate physical parameters such as the transmittance map and global atmospheric light. In the second stage, the strong generative capacity of the diffusion model is integrated with the physical priors obtained in the first stage, endowing the diffusion process with physical awareness to better compensate for information loss caused by clouds. Extensive experiments on both synthetic and real-world datasets demonstrate that the proposed method achieves competitive performance compared to state-of-the-art approaches.
author: Yuxin Zhu, Jun Li, Qinghong Sheng, Xiao Ling, Bo Wang, Fan Gao, Piji Li

title: Single Remote Sensing Image Super-Resolution via Convolutional Neural Network and Diffusion Model
content: Recent diffusion generative model super-resolution (SR) methods have made great progress in remote sensing image quality enhancement. However, the representation learning capability of diffusion models has not been fully explored, especially for photorealistic reconstruction using richer prior information. In this article, we propose an end-to-end remote sensing image SR method (convolutional neural network-guided diffusion model SR method, CNN-DiffSR). This method combines the initial SR image generated by a convolutional neural network (CNN) to guide the diffusion model SR reconstruction, generating visually clear and semantically accurate image samples that are closer to high-resolution images. Experiments on multiple benchmark datasets show that our method can simultaneously restore remote sensing images with more accurate texture details and high visual perception quality. In addition, more decent results and higher classification accuracy (+6.6%) are obtained in real-world remote sensing image SR experiments and downstream task performance improvement tests, indicating that the proposed method provides a potential solution for improving the readability of remote sensing images.
author: Fanen Meng, Zhiguo Jiang, Fengying Xie, Sensen Wu, Zhenhong Du, Haopeng Zhang

title: Detail-Aware Local and Context-Aware Global Dual-Branch Upsampling for Pan-Sharpening
content: While deep learning methods have demonstrated significant success in pan-sharpening, most existing approaches suffer from insufficient high-frequency guidance from panchromatic (PAN) images during the upsampling of low-resolution multispectral (LRMS) images, often failing to reconstruct fine-grained local details and maintain global contextual consistency. To alleviate these limitations, we propose a novel detail-aware local and context-aware global dual-branch upsampling (DCDU) method for pan-sharpening. The core of DCDU is a dual-branch architecture specifically designed to decouple and leverage both local structural details and global semantic information from both modalities: the detail-aware local branch calculates feature-space correlations between LRMS images and PAN images to derive adaptive kernels, guiding the reconstruction of local neighborhoods and facilitating high-fidelity injection of fine-grained spatial structures; the context-aware global branch mainly employs a cross-modality channel-specific spatial attention mechanism to holistically model long-range dependencies, dynamically recalibrating spectral-spatial representations based on cross-modal context and capturing macrolevel semantic coherence across the entire scene. The distinct information streams from the two branches are then integrated via an adaptive and learnable mapping function. We also provide a theoretical analysis under the minimum mean square error criterion to motivate our dual-stream strategy, the efficacy of which is robustly validated by our experimental results. As a generic and lightweight upsampling module, the collaborative design can be seamlessly integrated into various backbone networks, enabling superior performance across multiple datasets.
author: Boao Liu, Kai Sun, Jiangshe Zhang, Shuang Xu, Junmin Liu

title: Incorporating Housing Vacancy Constraints into Urban Expansion Simulations of Shrinking Mining Cities
content: Recent decades have witnessed the persistent population decline and rising number of housing vacancies in many resource-based cities, posing significant challenges to conventional urban growth theories and modeling approaches. To address the scientific shortcomings of traditional urban expansion models in capturing the “population loss–housing vacancy–spatial shrinkage” process, this study developed a method to simulate the spatial evolution of shrinking mining cities. First, nighttime light data were used to construct a “ghost city” index and identify trends in light variation, enabling the detection of existing and potential housing vacancy areas and development of a quantifiable indicator of housing vacancy zones. This indicator was systematically incorporated into a case-based reasoning (CBR) model as a key shrinkage driving factor to constrain the spatial priority of urban expansion. Using the central urban area of Jixi City as a case study, the improved CBR model achieved an overall accuracy of 96.01%, a Kappa coefficient of 0.89, and a figure of merit of 0.273, demonstrating its superiority over the traditional model. This allowed for a more realistic reproduction of infill expansion patterns constrained by population loss. The results show that integrating housing vacancy areas into the model effectively enhances the explanatory power and adaptability of spatial evolution simulations of shrinking cities. This research provides a novel quantitative method and offers scientific evidence to elucidate the mechanisms of spatial pattern evolution and optimize land use in resource-based cities.
author: Xin Ye, Heng Li, Chunsen Chen, Jianping Zhao, Xin Wang, Yutao Li, Lina Lv

title: Incremental Learning for Passive Microwave Precipitation Retrievals Using Advanced Technology Microwave Sounder
content: Spaceborne passive microwave (PMW) radiometry is central to global precipitation monitoring, yet retrieval uncertainties remain substantial, particularly for cross-track sounders whose variable footprints and channel configurations are optimized for atmospheric temperature and moisture profiling rather than precipitation. Consequently, existing operational products often exhibit angular-dependent biases, limited effective swath utilization, unrealistic rainfall probability distributions, and systematic misclassification of precipitation phase. These limitations are further compounded by the scarcity of globally accurate and representative precipitation observations, as training data from the dual-frequency precipitation radar (DPR) and the cloud profiling radar (CPR) are spatially sparse, lack uniform global coverage, and exhibit heterogeneous error characteristics across precipitation regimes. To address these challenges, this study presents a supervised retrieval algorithm that incrementally trains an ensemble of extreme gradient-boosted decision trees by augmenting base learners with pretraining on reanalysis data and post-training on coincident DPR and CPR observations matched with the advanced technology microwave sounder (ATMS). By transferring prior information from reanalysis to posterior constraints from radar observations and adopting a sequential detection–estimation strategy for precipitation phase and rate retrieval, the proposed approach yields retrievals across the full ATMS swath that are largely free from persistent deficiencies in current global precipitation measurement (GPM) PMW operational products. In particular, the method resolves bimodal artifacts in rainfall retrievals and mitigates systematic high-latitude snowfall biases, including overestimation across the Arctic and underestimation across the Antarctic. Validation against independent Multi-Radar/Multi-Sensor (MRMS) data over the Contiguous United States (CONUS) further demonstrates improved performance in precipitation phase detection and rate estimation relative to both reanalysis and current GPM PMW products.
author: Mahyar Garshasbi, Buddha Subedi, Ardeshir Ebtehaj, Lisa Milani, F. Joseph Turk, George J. Huffman

title: Automated Motile Marine Wildlife Detection in Coral Reefs Using Airborne Fluid Lensing
content: Coral reef ecosystems are rapidly changing due to global climate change, with marine heat waves and human stressors exerting significant pressure. This has led to the decline of marine biodiversity, most famously in corals, a keystone species that supports global ocean ecosystems. While there have been advancements in assessing benthic substrate biodiversity in reefs, the evaluation of motile marine wildlife biodiversity has lagged, with potential biases from in situ disturbances. NASA’s airborne fluid lensing technology, deployed on autonomous aircraft, has enabled three-dimensional imaging of coral reef ecosystems at the cm-scale to depths of 20 m, aiding in benthic biodiversity assessment without wave distortions. However, this technology removes transient artifacts, including motile marine life, which is crucial for quantifying biodiversity in shallow marine ecosystems. We develop a new airborne fluid lensing-based marine wildlife detection algorithm to characterize moving underwater wildlife using raw fluid lensing data and solutions. The detector assesses marine animals in time series imagery, deriving animal planform area, velocity, and abundance across depths of ∼0–10 m, at cm-scale resolution, in complex coral reef ecosystems over dozens of square kilometers. The algorithm’s accuracy and performance were evaluated using a consensus-based technique in a single-blind experiment. The datasets span three ecologically distinct coral reefs in Guam. Results indicate the algorithm detects marine wildlife three times more efficiently than humans, with 6% less precision. Its inconsistent recall, dropping to 10% at its worst, indicates sensitivity to environmental conditions. These high-quality results could greatly lower the cost of dataset curation for future machine learning methods.
author: Noah Pikielny, Jessie Yang, Shaili Johri, Ved Chirayath

title: Semantics-Guided Reconstruction of Weak-Manhattan Indoor as-Built BIMs From 3-D Point Clouds
content: The reconstruction of indoor as-built building information modeling (BIMs) from three-dimensional (3-D) point clouds has garnered considerable interest due to its significant potential in applications. However, the challenges posed by severely occluded and cluttered environments, along with the complexity of indoor building structures, complicate the automatic reconstruction task. To address these issues, we propose an effective pipeline for reconstructing weak-Manhattan indoor as-built BIMs directly from raw 3-D point clouds. Based on fine-grained semantics derived from our previous studies, we first develop a semantic-guided room segmentation method without the down-sample operations for fixed-size input, thus preventing the loss of room boundary details, especially for large-scale indoor scenes. Following this, we present a novel room-wise vectorization method that does not rely on strict Manhattan assumptions. This process allows us to achieve regular room borders while preserving their original shapes as much as possible. Finally, the principal building structures and in-wall elements are converted into a popup 3-D model, allowing for the subsequent assembly of comprehensive BIMs. To validate the reliability and robustness, our approach is conducted on the available S3DIS and Matterport3D datasets from both qualitative and quantitative perspectives. Results from our approach achieves higher performance than contemporary state-of-the-art methods, retaining finer boundary details and achieving decimeter-level modeling accuracy. Our code is publicly released at https://github.com/wangxuzhecode/FloorSG.
author: Xuzhe Wang, Juntao Yang, Zhizhong Kang, Yutao Zhang, Jianghong Zhao, Wen Xiao, Xue Zhang, Qingtian Zeng

title: The Impact of Different Ionospheric Mapping Functions on Modeling Global Ionospheric Vertical Total Electron Content
content: A key step in ionosphere modeling is to convert slant total electron content (STEC) to vertical total electron content (VTEC) using an appropriate ionospheric mapping function (MF). This makes the accuracy of the applied MF a major factor affecting the performance of the built ionospheric model. Thus, this study evaluates ionospheric models constructed with three commonly used MFs, i.e., single-layer mapping function (SLM MF), modified single-layer mapping function (MSLM MF), and Klobuchar MF, from different aspects. Results show that the performance of the ionospheric model estimated with MSLM MF is the best, followed by SLM MF and Klobuchar MF, respectively. Compared with global ionosphere maps provided by the international Global Navigation Satellite System (IGSG), the MSLM MF-based ionospheric model achieves the best accuracy and consistency. During high solar activity, the SLM MF-based ionospheric model shows the largest discrepancies, which is up to about 40 TEC unit (TECU), while those of the MSLM MF-based model reduce to 10–15 TECU. Under low solar activity, the differences decrease, with the MSLM MF-based model maintaining the smallest bias and root mean square (RMS), which are -1.43 TECU and 2.48 TECU, respectively. The largest errors appear in equatorial regions (±15° latitude), where strong ionospheric gradients exist. Over oceanic regions, the MSLM MF-based ionospheric model still performs best, keeping bias smaller than 5 TECU and RMS smaller than 6 TECU, while the errors of the SLM MF-based ionospheric model can reach 15 TECU. Self-consistency assessments further confirm the stability of the MSLM MF-based ionospheric model, with bias and RMS below 1.0 TECU and 2.0 TECU, significantly outperforming those of the SLM MF-based and Klobuchar MF-based ionospheric models. Furthermore, the single-frequency precise point positioning tests also indicate that MSLM MF-based ionospheric corrections yield the best positioning accuracy, with horizontal and vertical errors typically ranging from 0.2–0.7 m and 0.4–1.0 m, respectively. Overall, the MSLM MF demonstrates the most reliable and consistent performance in ionospheric modeling under varied solar conditions, across diverse regions, and according to multiple metrics.
author: Jun Chen, Jiahuan Hu, Pan Li, Xiaodong Ren

title: Interferometric Water Cloud Model for Forest Growing Stock Volume Estimation by Combining Statistical Methods With Backscatter/FVC Space
content: Forest growing stock volume (GSV) reflects both timber volume and carbon sequestration capacity, serving as a crucial parameter for assessing forest ecosystem functionality. Traditional GSV monitoring methods are costly, time-consuming, and incapable of rapid, large-scale surveillance. Microwave remote sensing technology offers advantages such as strong penetration capability and the ability to acquire data under all weather conditions and at any time, providing a feasible approach for large-scale forest monitoring. The interferometric water cloud model (IWCM), as a semi-empirical model for estimating forest GSV, has demonstrated good application results, but suffers from issues such as excessive parameters, low solution efficiency, and strong dependence on measured data. Therefore, we categorize IWCM parameters into backscatter parameters and coherence parameters. The backscattering parameters are solved by a feature space method constructed by backscattering coefficients and fractional vegetation cover based on the water cloud model, and the coherence parameters are determined by the least squares method. This approach reduces reliance on measured data, maintains the physical significance of parameters, and enhances solution efficiency. The final estimated results have a correlation coefficient of 0.49 with the National Forest Inventory data, a root mean square error (RMSE) of 114.88 m3/ha, and a relative RMSE of 79.00%. Finally, we conducted parameter convergence analysis and comparative experiments with backscattering and coherence fitting results to further verify the effectiveness of this method in estimating GSV.
author: Tian Zhang, Yajie Liu, Hao Sun, Daqing Ge, Rongyuan Liu, Man Li, Xiangxing Wan

title: Q-SOCO-Field: A Quantum-Enhanced Observation Capability Representation Framework for Earth Observation Sensor Planning
content: Efficient and reliable Earth observation (EO) sensor planning depends critically on a precise and fine-grained cognition of sensor observation capabilities, which encompass multidimensional attributes ranging from static parameters to high-frequency dynamic behaviors. However, classical representational paradigms, exemplified by the sensor observation capability object-field (SOCO-Field), face a fundamental scalability barrier: the escalating complexity that accompanies spatiotemporal refinement and the inefficient representation of time-varying observation capabilities. To overcome this bottleneck, this study introduces the Quantum-SOCO-Field (Q-SOCO-Field), a framework designed to innovatively represent and compute the observation capabilities of EO sensors within a quantum computing paradigm. Q-SOCO-Field leverages quantum superposition and entanglement to encode the observation capabilities of the entire dynamic scenario into a unified quantum state. This achieves an exponential reduction in representational complexity. Furthermore, it establishes a computational framework that integrates fundamental quantum operators with Grover's algorithm, enabling the efficient execution of heterogeneous observation capability queries essential for EO sensor planning. This achieves a quadratic speed-up for certain classes of computations. Validation in a flood monitoring-oriented sensor planning scenario demonstrates the framework's high efficacy and significant advantage in cognizing observation capabilities. Taken together, this work lays the foundation for quantum-enhanced spatiotemporal cognition, pointing toward more efficient and reliable EO sensor planning.
author: Jie Li, Xuan Ding, Ke Wang, Jialin Li, Bo Jia, Maocai Wang, Chuli Hu

title: MSP-MAE: Multiscale Perceptive Masked Autoencoder for Self-Supervised SAR Target Recognition
content: The masked autoencoder (MAE) is a self-supervised learning model that reconstructs masked patches of an input image. Its pretrained encoder significantly enhances downstream tasks, even surpassing supervised methods. This is particularly important for synthetic aperture radar (SAR) target recognition because labeled SAR images are scarce. Leveraging large amounts of unlabeled SAR data for network pretraining mitigates this challenge. Therefore, by combining the scale-fusion spatial-channel attention (SF-SCA) and multihead self-attention (MHSA) modules, the multiscale perceptive convolutional transformer (MSP-CT) is proposed as the backbone of the multiscale perceptive MAE (MSP-MAE) for SAR target recognition tasks. The MHSA module exhibits locality and sparsity in shallow features, thereby introducing redundancy in global dependence modeling. In contrast, the SF-SCA module facilitates the interaction of localized and sparse patches through a sliding window. Therefore, the MSP-CT architecture employs alternating stacks of SF-SCA and MHSA blocks to balance the ability of the model to extract local and global features. The experimental results show that MSP-MAE pretraining can effectively improve the performance of the downstream task and can greatly alleviate the problem of insufficient labeled samples.
author: Xunyang Wan, Xiaolin Du, Xiaolong Chen, Guolong Cui, Jibin Zheng, Mengjiao Tang, Wenming Ma, Kaige Hou, Jinwei Wang

title: ShipGen: Improving Remote Sensing Ship Detection via Layout-Conditioned Diffusion-Based Data Augmentation
content: Synthetic aperture radar ship detection plays a key role in the field of remote sensing. Data augmentation is frequently used to enhance detector performance due to the common challenge of limited annotated data. However, most augmented methods are limited to geometric transformations, “copy–paste,” or image inpainting, which are guided by strict constraints and suffer limited target and background diversity. To address this, we propose a novel multiphase transfer-learned framework with layout-conditioned diffusion model. It incorporates sparse target layouts as relaxed conditions into the latent diffusion model, where the conditions take the form of bounding boxes that specify target positions and sizes. This leaves other ship attributes (e.g., orientation and scattering characteristics) and backgrounds to be freely generated and enhance target and background diversity. For enhancing the model's convergence on small datasets, we first pretrain it on large-scale datasets, and then, fine-tune it on small-scale ones for transfer learning. This enables precise layout-guided target generation even when using small datasets. For subsequent data augmentation, we adopt the widely used grounded-language-to-image generation model for practical use, and based on the transfer-learned model, we further develop a new automatic augmentation pipeline with a filtering mechanism. This pipeline integrates multiple foundation models and tools, including layout augmentation, segment-anything-model-based annotation correction, and data filtering, and can automatically adjust inappropriate annotations and screen high-quality data to serve as augmented data for downstream detection tasks. Experiments on SSDD and HRSID datasets validate that the pipeline improves multiple detectors' performance across in-shore and off-shore scenarios, and qualitative analyses confirm high diversity and quality of generated images.
author: Yuhan Zhang, Jun Liao, Jie Zhou, Yuanpei Chen, Xiaode Liu, Weihang Peng, Hongyu Yang, Qi Ming, Yufei Guo

title: SCENet: Semantic Consistency-Enhanced Decoding With Cross-Scale Tokens for High-Resolution Remote Sensing Image Segmentation
content: Semantic segmentation of high-resolution remote sensing imagery still faces significant challenges in complex urban scenes, with one critical yet frequently overlooked issue being the semantic drift and structural degradation phenomena occurring during the progressive multiscale feature reconstruction in the decoding phase. Due to the substantial disparity in semantic granularity between shallow and deep features, unconstrained progressive upsampling tends to progressively deviate decoded features from high-level semantic cognition, resulting in blurred boundaries and fragmented small objects. To addresses this phenomenon, this article models the decoding process as a semantic reconstruction process constrained by deep semantic anchoring, and proposes a Semantic Consistency Enhancement Network. This method stabilizes the semantic orientation of decoded features through a Semantic Token-guided Query–Context Alignment mechanism, and utilizes the Semantic Reconstruction Module to jointly model cross-scale semantic discrepancies and spatial structure recovery, progressively reconstructing high-fidelity spatial structures while maintaining semantic consistency. Furthermore, cross-decoding-stage semantic consistency regularization constraints are introduced to suppress semantic drift from the internal representation perspective without increasing inference overhead. Extensive experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method significantly improves segmentation accuracy and boundary quality on multiple high-resolution remote sensing datasets.
author: Chiyu Liu, Haiyan Du, Zhaolong Gao

title: DFF-Net: A Dual-Branch Feature Fusion Network for River Ice Mapping via the Fusion of Microwave and Optical Imagery
content: River ice mapping in high-altitude cold mountainous regions is critical for understanding ice processes and preventing disasters. However, persistent cloud and snow cover often impede accurate extraction from optical satellite imagery. To overcome these challenges, a deep learning model for river ice extraction named the dual-branch feature fusion network (DFF-Net), which is based on multifeatured fusion and an attention mechanism, is proposed in this study. In this model, through the addition of different attention mechanisms, features are effectively extracted from multisource data, including optical imagery, synthetic aperture radar data, the normalized difference snow index (NDSI), and a digital elevation model. These features are synergistically fused at both the feature and decision levels. We conducted comparative experiments on DFF-Net, NDSI, relative difference river ice (RDRI), and U-Net in clear-sky, cloud-covered, and snow-covered scenarios. The results indicate that DFF-Net achieves the best performance across all the considered accuracy metrics. Compared with those of NDSI, RDRI, and U-Net, its mean intersection over union (mIoU) in the clear-sky scenario increased by 0.1198, 0.0561, and 0.045, respectively; in the cloud-covered scenario, its mIoU improved by 0.2563, 0.2959, and 0.0613, respectively; and in the snow-covered scenario, its mIoU improved by 0.2542, 0.179, and 0.073, respectively. Furthermore, the mean boundary error of DFF-Net across all three scenarios was only 3.599 pixels, thus demonstrating outstanding overall performance. The DFF-Net model effectively integrates the strengths of multiple features, thereby significantly increasing the accuracy of river ice extraction with cloud and snow distributions, and it demonstrated a strong ability to map river ice extent over large spatial scales. This study provides a reliable technical solution for high-frequency river ice monitoring in high-altitude cold mountainous regions and can support river ice research in cold regions.
author: Haojie Li, Yuzhong Wang, Zhen Liu, Liankun Huang, Donghang Shao, Tianwen Feng, Xiaohua Hao

title: Domain knowledge constrained dynamic graph learning of GNSS sensor networks for spatio-temporal slope deformation monitoring
